Safety Apr 11, 2026

Safe Than Sorry: British Airways Airbus A380 Lands Too Late, Performs Touch & Go In London

The incident is certainly rather unusual.

A380's late descent approach forced the crew to execute a touch-and-go maneuver, highlighting how weight-and-balance calculations and approach planning directly impact large-frame operations at congested airports.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

The Longest US Airline Routes Flown By The Boeing 787 Dreamliner

Some flights push passengers — and aircraft — to their absolute limits. Here is where US airlines fly the Boeing 787 the furthest.

Ultra-long-range 787 operations expose fuel efficiency advantages that enable new point-to-point markets previously requiring hub connections, reshaping network planning strategy.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 11, 2026

Exclusive: Interview with the Chief Test Pilot of VMX-1

On the occasion of the MCAS Yuma airshow 2026, we had the opportunity to interview the Chief Operational Test Director and Chief Test Pilot of Marine Operational Test & Evaluation Squadron (VMX) 1. After speaking last year with the Commanding Officer of VMX-1, Colonel John “Ike” Dirk, we realized...

VMX-1's chief test pilot insights reveal how Marine Corps operational testing evolves to validate advanced aircraft capabilities for fleet integration.

See The Aviationist →

Corporate Apr 11, 2026

The MileagePlus Overhaul That Splits United Airlines Flyers Into Credit Card Haves & Have-Nots

A big change has arrived for MileagePlus members, and it may well change the meaning of United's rewards forever.

United's MileagePlus restructuring creates a two-tier loyalty system where credit card holders gain premium earning advantages, potentially widening the gap between casual and committed frequent flyers.

See Simple Flying →

Corporate Apr 11, 2026

United Airlines Sued Over Firing 76-Year-Old Aircraft Tech Who Took Water While Feeling Ill

A 76-year-old aircraft technician's termination over drinking water while feeling unwell has sparked a lawsuit against United Airlines.

United's termination of an aging aircraft technician for a minor wellness action raises employment liability risks and potentially conflicts with age discrimination protections in safety-sensitive roles.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Regulatory Apr 11, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Organizations must document their rationale for accepting residual risks after implementing controls, creating accountability that regulators will scrutinize during compliance audits.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

The Aircraft Set To Replace United Airlines' Iconic Boeing 757s

A new era is dawning as United Airlines prepares to bid farewell to an icon, but what will take its place?

United's 757 replacement strategy will likely prioritize fuel efficiency and range capabilities to maintain its long-haul domestic and transatlantic network competitiveness.

See Simple Flying →

International Apr 11, 2026

Dublin Airport warns passengers about protest delays on M50

On April 10, Dublin Airport (DUB) issued warnings urging passengers to allow extra time for their journey as the current national fuel protest will cause delays. The commuting routes into Dublin will be affected and other major roads around the country. Passengers warned of major delays in...

Airport ground transportation disruptions from external protests can significantly impact passenger arrival times and operational efficiency, requiring advance communication strategies to minimize missed flights.

See Travel Radar →

Military Apr 11, 2026

Backtrack? Why Germany May Double Its F-35 Order & Abandon Europe's Own Fighter Jet

As Germany weighs the pros and cons of expanding its F-35 fleet, the decision could have far-reaching implications for Europe's defense landscape.

Germany's potential F-35 expansion signals European defense consolidation around U.S. platforms, threatening viability of the continent's €100B+ joint fighter development programs.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 11, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

Operators must formally document their Safety Management System processes to demonstrate regulatory compliance and reduce audit findings during FAA inspections.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

Falcon 9 launches Cygnus cargo spacecraft to the ISS

A Falcon 9 launched a Cygnus cargo spacecraft April 11 as Northrop Grumman continues its dependence on a competitor to fly resupply missions to the International Space Station.

Northrop Grumman's reliance on SpaceX launch services highlights the commercial space industry's consolidation around dominant providers, creating potential supply chain vulnerabilities for critical ISS logistics.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Apr 11, 2026

I’m A Flight Attendant: This Is What We Notice About Passengers Immediately

Flight attendants are always monitoring their passengers.

Flight attendants' real-time passenger observations serve as critical safety assets beyond standard security protocols, enhancing threat detection capabilities.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

How Ryanair Enforces Cabin Baggage Rules

Air travel in Europe has become synonymous with low fares, but also with increasingly strict baggage rules. Ryanair, in particular, has turned enforcement into a highly visible part of the flying experience. According to the BBC and The Guardian, ground staff earn €2.50 for every piece of oversized...

Ryanair's €2.50-per-violation commission for ground staff creates financial incentives that may drive inconsistent enforcement and customer friction beyond stated baggage policies.

See Travel Radar →

▶
General Aviation Apr 11, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Regional airports like CID serve as critical hubs for flight training pipelines while maintaining commercial carrier operations, making them essential to workforce development amid pilot shortages.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

La NASA da la bienvenida a la Tierra a los exploradores lunares de Artemis II, quienes batieron récords

Los primeros astronautas en viajar a la Luna en más de medio siglo han regresado a la Tierra tras una misión que ha establecido nuevos récords a bordo del vuelo de prueba Artemis II de la NASA. Los astronautas de la NASA Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover y Christina Koch, y el astronauta Jeremy Hansen de...

Artemis II's successful crewed lunar mission validates NASA's deep-space architecture for sustained lunar exploration and establishes operational baselines for future Mars missions.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

KLM's Record UK Expansion: Fly To The World From These 19 Local Airports

The Dutch flag carrier has a huge presence in the country.

KLM's 19-airport UK network strategy positions the carrier to capture connecting traffic from regional airports that traditionally feed into London hubs.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

Here's What Pilots Notice First When Switching Between The Airbus A350 & Boeing 787 Cockpits

Comparing the flight decks of the world's two most advanced widebody airliners.

The A350's side-stick control and integrated flight deck differ fundamentally from the 787's traditional yoke, requiring substantial pilot retraining and affecting operational procedures across airlines.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Safety Apr 11, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strikes remain a leading fatal helicopter hazard despite pilot overconfidence in reaction time, necessitating enhanced training and operational awareness protocols.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

NASA Science, Cargo Launch Aboard Northrop Grumman CRS-24

NASA is sending more science, technology demonstrations, and crew supplies to the International Space Station following the successful launch of the agency’s Northrop Grumman Commercial Resupply Services 24 mission, or Northrop Grumman CRS-24. Northrop Grumman’s Cygnus XL spacecraft, carrying...

Northrop Grumman's expanded Cygnus XL variant demonstrates the commercial space industry's increasing capacity to support ISS operations with larger payload volumes.

See NASA →

Military Apr 11, 2026

How The F-16 Stacks Up Against The MiG-35 In 2026

How does the Viper compare to the Super Fulcrum?

The F-16 Viper's proven multi-role capability and extensive allied integration network provide strategic advantage over the MiG-35's advanced avionics in contested airspace scenarios.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Apr 11, 2026

Fast-growing RAVE Aerospace identifies industry choke points

Enjoying robust demand for its RAVE series of embedded inflight entertainment systems, RAVE Aerospace has identified widespread memory chip shortages and safety regulators’ more stringent head injury criterion (HIC) certification requirements as among the challenges presently faced by aircraft...

Memory chip scarcity and stricter HIC certification standards are creating production bottlenecks for IFE systems despite strong market demand.

See Runway Girl Network →

Industry Apr 11, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation sectors offers immediate efficiency gains that can accommodate projected traffic growth without proportional environmental impact increases.

See MDPI →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

Lufthansa Strike Disrupts Flights at Frankfurt and Munich Hubs

Lufthansa is facing new disruptions as cabin crew walk out for the third time in two months, grounding flights across its main German hubs, Frankfurt and Munich. The strike on Friday, April 10, 2026, affected services scheduled between 12:00 and 22:00 (CEST), with widespread cancellations expected....

Repeated labor actions at Europe's largest airline hubs signal escalating wage disputes that threaten to become a structural operational risk beyond isolated disruption events.

See Travel Radar →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

Artemis II Splashes Down

This image from April 10, 2026, captures NASA’s Orion spacecraft, with its parachutes deployed, seconds before splashdown in the Pacific Ocean. The Artemis II crew accomplished many milestones on their nearly 10-day mission, surpassing the Apollo 13 record for farthest crewed spaceflight and...

Artemis II's successful splashdown validates Orion's crewed return systems, clearing NASA's path for lunar landing missions and demonstrating critical human spaceflight recovery capabilities.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

American Airlines Join US Airlines Raising Fees

U.S. airlines are passing rising fuel costs on to travellers through higher baggage fees, with American Airlines and Alaska Air among the latest carriers to raise prices. This latest moves come as jet fuel prices have surged sharply since February, driven by conflict in the Middle East and wider...

Airlines are using baggage fees as a primary mechanism to offset fuel surcharge impacts while maintaining published ticket prices, shifting cost visibility to ancillary revenue.

See Travel Radar →

Military Apr 11, 2026

New Images Provide Clearest View Yet of Damage to U.S.-made AN/FPS-132 Early Warning Radar in Qatar

The imagery offers a closer look at the damage to the giant phased-array AN/FPS-132 radar struck during Iran’s attack on Qatar last month. Newly published photos by Al Jazeera circulated online offer the clearest view yet t0 the damage sustained by the U.S.-made AN/FPS-132 Block 5 (SSPARS)...

Detailed damage assessment of the AN/FPS-132 radar reveals the vulnerability of critical air defense infrastructure to modern strike capabilities, highlighting gaps in regional detection coverage.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Apr 11, 2026

JetSMART Introduces “All You Can Fly” Subscription Model

JetSMART is reshaping low-cost travel in South America with the launch of its new “All You Can Fly” membership. The airline announced the plan on April 9, offering passengers unlimited flights for a fixed annual fee. A New Way to Travel More for Less This plan allows travellers to pay once and...

JetSMART's unlimited-flights subscription model directly challenges traditional low-cost carrier economics in Latin America, requiring aggressive route expansion and load factor management to remain profitable.

See Travel Radar →

International Apr 11, 2026

Separate Operations in Oman Air’s Acquisition of SalamAir

SalamAir has been acquired by Oman’s government as part of an integration process. According to the government, the acquisition aims to form a “sustainable national aviation sector,” though Oman Air and SalamAir will remain independent in both operations and branding for the time being. The origins...

Oman Air's acquisition of SalamAir maintains dual-brand operations, signaling a consolidation strategy that preserves market segmentation while centralizing ownership for long-term sector sustainability.

See Travel Radar →

General Aviation Apr 11, 2026

AOPA presents draft governance changes to members

AOPA presented a set of bylaw changes to members, seeking feedback on a set of significant potential revisions, including changes to the process for nominating members to the Board of Trustees, setting term limits, and establishing required trustee qualifications to serve on the Board.

AOPA's proposed trustee qualifications and term limits signal a shift toward institutionalizing board governance standards that could influence how aviation advocacy organizations balance member representation with professional management.

See AOPA News →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

NASA Welcomes Record-Setting Artemis II Moonfarers Back to Earth

The first astronauts to travel to the Moon in more than half a century are back on Earth after a record-setting mission aboard NASA’s Artemis II test flight. NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen splashed down at...

Artemis II's successful crew return validates NASA's deep-space architecture for sustained lunar exploration, establishing operational confidence for crewed Mars missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

Moog Technology Keeps Artemis II Astronauts Safe During Historic Lunar Mission

East Aurora, NY – Moog Inc. (NYSE: MOG.A and MOG.B), a worldwide designer, manufacturer, and systems integrator of high-performance precision motion and fluid controls and control systems, highlights the critical […]...

Moog's precision motion and fluid control systems are essential subsystems enabling crewed lunar missions, demonstrating how specialized suppliers are integral to deep-space exploration programs.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 11, 2026

Orion splashes down to successfully end Artemis 2 mission

The first human mission beyond Earth orbit in more than 50 years successfully concluded with a splashdown in the Pacific Ocean April 10.

Artemis 2's successful return validates NASA's deep-space capsule systems for crewed lunar missions, establishing operational confidence for the Moon landing attempt in Artemis 3.

See SpaceNews →

International Apr 10, 2026

The Role of Aviation in Humanitarian Aid and Disaster Relief

The United Nations Humanitarian Air Service (UNHAS) managed by the World Food Programme (WFP) is expected to play a vital role in 2026, serving as a necessary link for aid workers and relief supplies in some of the world’s most remote and dangerous locations. What is Humanitarian Aviation?...

UNHAS operations in 2026 will demonstrate aviation's irreplaceable value in reaching conflict zones and remote areas where ground infrastructure is compromised or nonexistent.

See Travel Radar →

Military Apr 10, 2026

Swedish Armed Forces Release Photo Showing JAS 39 Gripens Escorting Russian Kilo-Class Submarine

The Russian submarine shadowed by the Swedish Gripens was transiting the Kattegat, a strategic waterway linking the Baltic approaches with the North Sea system. Although the spotlight is currently on Iran, the Russian military activity in the Baltic region continues to draw scrutiny. The Swedish...

Sweden's interception of Russian submarine transits through the Kattegat demonstrates NATO's increased air-maritime coordination in the Baltic, signaling escalated monitoring of Russian naval movements in strategically critical waterways.

See The Aviationist →

Air Cargo Apr 10, 2026

Cathay Cargo Launches New Manage Booking System

Cathay Cargo has upgraded its booking system, giving customers direct control over their bookings. This system, called Manage Booking, is available 24/7 on their online platform. The update has been made to allow for instant changes post-booking rather than needing to follow up with the company....

Self-service booking management reduces administrative overhead for cargo customers while improving operational efficiency through real-time booking modifications.

See Travel Radar →

General Aviation Apr 10, 2026

Virginia Airport Rebrand Draws Local Opposition

Manassas weighs adding “Washington” to airport name ahead of planned airline service.

Adding 'Washington' to Manassas Airport's name aims to leverage metro-area branding for commercial airline service viability, but local resistance suggests community concerns about regional identity loss may complicate the airport's growth strategy.

See AVweb →

Adding 'Washington' to Manassas Airport's name aims to leverage metro-area branding for commercial airline service viability, but local resistance suggests community concerns about regional identity loss may complicate the airport's growth strategy.
